rows.item: 学習意欲・授業参加度

summary:
Overview: Global English and Communication
This course introduces you to the diverse nature of English, as in Englishes. As English is becoming a world language, the dominant language by which much of the business and science world functions, it has become imperative for us to openly address what this widespread phenomena means. We humans are using it in different ways for different purposes and we need to catch up with its variations so as participate successfully as global citizens. Some key questions informing this course are: What has prompted this surge towards English? How are globalizing currents propelling English forward? What issues in communication arise as we encounter people who speak English differently from us? Should we be moving towards certain ‘standards of usage’ regarding English use so that we can all understand each other? In what ways are we called on to ‘listen’ differently? We will wrestle with these questions as best we can?
goals: Students will develop a clear understanding on the concept of a lingua franca in general, and of English as a Lingua Franca specifically
Students will develop a clear understanding of the reasons why English has developed as an international language
Students will develop a clear understanding of the concept of World Englishes, and of how English is changed and transformed as it is adopted as a second language in different contexts.
Students will develop a clear understanding of the different contexts in which English is used for communication, including in business and academia.
Students will develop a clear understanding of English-based pidgin and creoles languages spoken in various parts of the world, and the factors that led to the development of these languages.
Students will develop a clear understanding of strategies that can be used to facilitate communication in global contexts

plan.text:
Introduction
LECTURE: Introducing World Englishes; Who speaks English?
Communications strategies workshop: Tell me more (Using longer, more complex utterances as a means of extending conversation )
Warm-up
Strategies
Practice
(Listening exercises)
Pair Work

Week 2. The native speaker model, comfortable intelligibility and comprehensibility
Communications strategies workshop: Ask for more (Asking follow-up questions as a means of extending conversations)
Warm-up
Strategies
Practice
(Listening exercises)
Pair Work

LECTURE: English in Asia and Europe; pidgins and creoles
Communications strategies workshop: Give me a sign! (Attending skills (i.e showing a speaker that your are listening/Verbal cues and rejoinders i.e short utterances in response to a speaker
Warm-up
Strategies
Practice
(Listening exercises)
Pair Work

LECTURE: A case study of ELF: Aviation English
Communications strategies workshop: Show me what you mean (Attending skills
Non-verbal cues i.e physical actions in response to a speaker/ Miming i.e making oneself understood through gestures)
Warm-up
Strategies
Practice
(Listening exercises)
Pair Work

LECTURE: English and international teachers
Communications strategies workshop: Are you with me? (Agreeing and disagreeing as a means of extending conversation/Giving an opinion)
Warm-up
Strategies
Practice
(Listening exercises)
Pair Work

LECTURE: English in Africa
Communications strategies workshop: How sure are you? Expressing assuredness (hedging statements based on one’s confidence in their accuracy)
Warm-up
Strategies
Practice
(Listening exercises)
Pair Work

Week 7. Englishes in America; South East Asia
Conversation Strategies Review (Units 1-6)
LECTURE: English and technology; New Englishes

LECTURE: English as a lingua franca
Communications strategies workshop: In other words (Approximating (i.e substituting one word for another with a similar meaning to overcome a language gap)

LECTURE: Native Englishes. A world apart!
Communications strategies workshop: Make it up! (Word coinage (i.e inventing a word to overcome a language gap)

LECTURE: Cultural hurdles to ELF and intercultural accommodation competence
Communications strategies workshop: Describe it! Circumlocution (i.e using a phrase to communicate what could also be described by a word to overcome language gap)

LECTURE: Japanese English
Communications strategies workshop: Say it another way (Rephrasing i.e saying an utterance a different way to overcome a language gap)

LECTURE: English. Convergence or Divergence?
Conversation Strategies Review (Units 8-11)

LECTURE: The Impact of English on Language Diversity?
Conversation Strategies Review (Units 8-11)
LECTURE: The Road ahead: English in the 21st Century

textbooks:
Readings from Understanding English Across Cultures (Honna et al, 2012)
PAIRWORK COMMUNICATION ACTIVITIES HANDOUTS WILL BE PROVIDED BY INSTRUCTOR
prereq: Students are requested to make notes on the lecture content, which they may do either in a notebook or on PC.
prep: Students are required to complete weekly readings on various themes connected with the course content. The time taken to complete these readings and answer the accompanying questions will depend on the speed of the reader

extra.text:
Overview:
This course introduces you to the diverse nature of English, as in Englishes. As English is becoming a world language, the dominant language by which much of the business and science world functions, it has become imperative for us to openly address what this widespread phenomena means. We humans are using it in different ways for different purposes and we need to catch up with its variations so as participate successfully as global citizens. Some key questions informing this course are: What has prompted this surge towards English? How are globalizing currents propelling English forward? What issues in communication arise as we encounter people who speak English differently from us? Should we be moving towards certain ‘standards of usage’ regarding English use so that we can all understand each other? In what ways are we called on to ‘listen’ differently? We will wrestle with these questions as best we can?
The instructor has 25 years of experience in teaching English as a Foreign Language to students in Japan, and is also a specialist in the development on the English language and its evolution as a global language.

extra.label: 成績評価に関する補足
extra.text:
This course uses a formative system of evaluation. Participants are expected to complete weekly online reading assignments based on the 15 units in the course reader (each quiz is ~5%), as well as the assigned in-class activities.
このコースでは形成評価システムを採用しています。参加者は、コースリーダーの15のユニットに基づく毎週のオンライン読書課題（各小テストは5%程度）と、授業内で割り当てられた課題を完了することが求められます。
